title: The Advanced RAG Toolkit
concept: Query rewriting, hybrid search, reranking, and self-verification as layered improvements on the naive pipeline.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we looked at why Naive RAG systems fail. What were the primary failure modes of a naive pipeline?

options.text: They cost too much to run and require specialized GPU hardware.
They often retrieve irrelevant information, miss multi-hop facts, and fail to verify if the answer is grounded.
They can only process text in English and cannot understand other languages.

exercises.explain: Naive RAG systems struggle with irrelevant chunks, missed multi-hop facts, and hallucinated answers because they lack pre-processing of queries and post-retrieval verification.

cards.title: Upgrading Our Pipeline
cards.body: Knowing that basic RAG retrieves messy, out-of-order chunks and hallucinated answers, how do we transform this fragile pipeline into a smart, self-correcting assistant? We use a specialized toolkit designed to clean, search, sort, and verify data at every stage.

cards.title: The Advanced RAG Toolkit
cards.body: The Advanced RAG Toolkit is a set of layered techniques—including query rewriting, hybrid search, reranking, and self-verification—designed to fix the errors, irrelevance, and gaps common in naive retrieval systems.

cards.title: The Research Assistant Analog
cards.body: Think of Naive RAG as a basic library search where you type one keyword and read whatever random book is on the top shelf. Advanced RAG is like having a research assistant who rewrites your messy query, searches both the catalog and the computer system, reorganizes the stack by relevance, and double-checks the final report for errors.

cards.title: An Advanced Pipeline in Action
cards.body: A user asks 'What's the cost of our premium plan?'. An advanced RAG system rewrites this to search for 'enterprise pricing tiers', pulls data using both keywords and vector embeddings, reranks the results to put the pricing sheet first, and verifies the LLM's draft answer against that sheet.

cards.title: The Big Model Myth
cards.body: Many believe that simply using a larger LLM will fix bad retrieval. In reality, if your search step retrieves wrong or messy information, even the smartest LLM will output incorrect answers. Better search tools, not just bigger models, are key.

cards.title: Naive vs. Advanced Flow
cards.body: Compare how Naive RAG and Advanced RAG handle incoming user questions and the search database.

left.text: Searches the user's raw, unoptimized query immediately using a single search method and trusts the first output.

right.text: Rewrites query, runs hybrid keyword/semantic search, reranks results, and verifies the response against sources.

exercises.prompt: What is hybrid search in the context of Advanced RAG?

options.text: It searches both internal company files and public Google search results simultaneously.
It combines semantic vector search with keyword-based search to catch both concepts and exact terms.
It requires two different LLMs to generate answers at the same time.
It alternates between searching text documents and searching image databases.

exercises.explain: Hybrid search combines dense vector search (which captures conceptual meaning) with sparse keyword search (which captures exact terms, names, and IDs) to get the best of both worlds.

exercises.prompt: Self-verification is a technique where the LLM cross-checks its final answer against the retrieved documents to catch hallucinations before showing the user.
exercises.explain: Self-verification allows the LLM to inspect its own generated answer against the source documents to ensure it hasn't introduced hallucinations or false facts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of sorting retrieved chunks:
exercises.sentence: To ensure the absolute best documents are at the top of the context window, we use reranking to re-order the retrieved chunks.
exercises.explain: Reranking acts as a secondary filter, evaluating the semantic relevance of retrieved documents more deeply than initial fast vector searches.

exercises.prompt: A developer needs to help their RAG system find exact product codes and matching database schemas. Which solution represents an AI-engineered Advanced RAG approach?

options.text: We can just write a quick python script to look for the word 'database' and highlight it in yellow before the LLM reads it.
Implement hybrid search combining dense vector embeddings with sparse BM25 retrieval, followed by a cross-encoder reranker to surface the most relevant database schemas.
Ask the user to type in their SQL query directly so that the system doesn't have to search through text files at all.

exercises.explain: This option generates a response using advanced RAG terminology (hybrid search, dense embeddings, lexical BM25, and cross-encoder reranker) to solve the exact problem of vocabulary mismatch.

exercises.prompt: If you were designing a robust, production-grade Advanced RAG pipeline, in what order would a user's question typically be processed to guarantee the most accurate answer?

options.text: It immediately queries the vector database and generates a response without altering the input.
It rewrites the query, runs a hybrid search, reranks the top results, and verifies the generated output against the source.
It bypasses vector search entirely to rely only on keyword matching and self-verification.
It sends the user's query straight to the LLM and only uses RAG if the LLM expresses confusion.

exercises.explain: Advanced RAG acts like a multi-step pipeline where the query is optimized first, multiple search strategies gather the raw results, a reranker filters for high quality, and a final self-verification step ensures the LLM's output actually matches the source documents.
